narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, LSAT score, family background, and other details to forecast your future performance. For you specifically, it calculated a 61.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which crossed the threshold for rejection—meaning the school sees a higher risk that you might not succeed right away after graduation. The primary reason for this prediction boils down to your grades in later undergraduate years, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting failure. Your Year 3 decile rank of 2—well below the typical student's 5.5—signaled to the model a concerning dip in performance compared to peers, as stronger finishes often correlate with bar success. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 35.5, slightly under the average of 36.7, but this actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, showing the model still credits your potential there. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of about 5.7, added further weight toward the failure prediction, highlighting an early pattern that didn't improve enough. While your middle family income bracket and full-time student status align with most applicants, they didn't sway the outcome much in this case. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having solid but not standout undergrad GPA around the 3.2 average, yet the lower decile ranks set you apart in a way that amplified concerns for first-time bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your character or determination—many students from similar backgrounds overcome these metrics through targeted effort. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost that score above average, strengthening your later-year grades perhaps through post-bac courses or explaining any dips in a new personal statement, and consider programs with bar prep support tailored for diverse applicants like yourself. With these steps, you can reapply stronger and shift the model's view in your favor.
